exercises.prompt: Which primary types have at least 50 Pokémon? Return type1 and the count as pokemon_count, most common first, breaking ties alphabetically by type1.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT type1, COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50 ORDER BY pokemon_count DESC, type1
hints: A count-per-group filter goes in HAVING, not WHERE — the count only exists after grouping.
GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50, then ORDER BY pokemon_count DESC, type1.

```sql
SELECT type1, COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50 ORDER BY pokemon_count DESC, type1
```

exercises.prompt: Which generations have an average total stat score above 430? Return generation and the average as avg_total rounded to 2 decimal places, ordered by generation ascending. Compare the true (unrounded) average against 430.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT generation, ROUND(AVG(total), 2) AS avg_total FROM pokemon GROUP BY generation HAVING AVG(total) > 430 ORDER BY generation
hints: Filter groups by their average with HAVING; round only in the SELECT for display.
GROUP BY generation HAVING AVG(total) > 430, and select ROUND(AVG(total), 2).

```sql
SELECT generation, ROUND(AVG(total), 2) AS avg_total FROM pokemon GROUP BY generation HAVING AVG(total) > 430 ORDER BY generation
```

exercises.prompt: Which primary types have an average attack of at least 90? Return type1 and the average as avg_attack rounded to 2 decimal places, highest average first, breaking ties alphabetically by type1. Compare the true (unrounded) average against 90.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT type1, ROUND(AVG(attack), 2) AS avg_attack FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING AVG(attack) >= 90 ORDER BY avg_attack DESC, type1
hints: HAVING can test AVG the same way it tests COUNT — group first, then keep groups whose average clears the bar.
GROUP BY type1 HAVING AVG(attack) >= 90, then ORDER BY avg_attack DESC, type1.

```sql
SELECT type1, ROUND(AVG(attack), 2) AS avg_attack FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING AVG(attack) >= 90 ORDER BY avg_attack DESC, type1
```

exercises.prompt: Among non-legendary Pokémon, which generations contain more than 100 species? Return generation and the count as species_count, ordered by generation ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT generation, COUNT(*) AS species_count FROM pokemon WHERE NOT is_legendary GROUP BY generation HAVING COUNT(*) > 100 ORDER BY generation
hints: WHERE removes the legendaries first, then GROUP BY forms generation groups, then HAVING keeps the big ones.
WHERE NOT is_legendary, GROUP BY generation HAVING COUNT(*) > 100, ORDER BY generation.

```sql
SELECT generation, COUNT(*) AS species_count FROM pokemon WHERE NOT is_legendary GROUP BY generation HAVING COUNT(*) > 100 ORDER BY generation
```

exercises.prompt: Which primary types have at least one Pokémon with a total stat score of 680 or more? Return type1 and the group's highest total as max_total, largest first, breaking ties alphabetically by type1.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT type1, MAX(total) AS max_total FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING MAX(total) >= 680 ORDER BY max_total DESC, type1
hints: "Has at least one member above a threshold" is a test on the group's MAX — put it in HAVING.
GROUP BY type1 HAVING MAX(total) >= 680, then ORDER BY max_total DESC, type1.

```sql
SELECT type1, MAX(total) AS max_total FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING MAX(total) >= 680 ORDER BY max_total DESC, type1
```

exercises.prompt: Which primary types have at least 30 Pokémon and an average speed above 70? Return type1, the count as pokemon_count, and the average speed as avg_speed rounded to 2 decimal places — fastest average first, breaking ties alphabetically by type1. Compare the true (unrounded) average against 70.
exercises.referenceSql: SELECT type1, COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count, ROUND(AVG(speed), 2) AS avg_speed FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30 AND AVG(speed) > 70 ORDER BY avg_speed DESC, type1
hints: HAVING can combine several aggregate tests with AND — both must hold for the group to survive.
GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30 AND AVG(speed) > 70, then ORDER BY avg_speed DESC, type1.

```sql
SELECT type1, COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count, ROUND(AVG(speed), 2) AS avg_speed FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30 AND AVG(speed) > 70 ORDER BY avg_speed DESC, type1
```
